name: Mark

unions.name: Item
unions.comment:
**Note** `index` is our position within the nested list.

The first `Int` in the tuple is our current position in the current sub list.

The `List Int` that follows are the indices for the parent list.

For example, given this list

```markup
|> List
    1. First element
    -- Second Element
        1. Element #2.1
            -- Element #2.1.1
        -- Element #2.2
    -- Third Element
```

here are the indices:

```markup
1. (1, [])
-- (2, [])
    1. (1, [2])
        -- (1, [1,2])
    -- (2, [2])
-- (3, [])
```
cases: { index : ( Basics.Int, List.List Basics.Int ), content : List.List item, children : Mark.Enumerated item }

aliases.name: Styles
aliases.type: { bold : Basics.Bool, italic : Basics.Bool, strike : Basics.Bool }

values.name: annotation
values.comment:
An annotation is some _styled text_, a _name_, and zero or more _attributes_.

So, we can make a `link` that looks like this in markup:

```markup
Here is my [*cool* sentence]{link| url = website.com }.
```

and rendered in elm-land via:

    link =
        Mark.annotation "link"
            (\id styles url ->
                Html.a
                    [ Html.Attributes.href url ]
                    (List.map renderStyles styles)
            )
            |> Mark.field "url" Mark.string
values.type: String.String -> (Mark.Internal.Id.Id -> List.List ( Mark.Styles, String.String ) -> result) -> Mark.Record result

values.name: balanced
values.comment:
A balanced replacement. This is used for replacing parentheses or to do auto-curly quotes.

    Mark.balanced
        { start = ( "\"", "“" )
        , end = ( "\"", "”" )
        }
values.type: { start : ( String.String, String.String ), end : ( String.String, String.String ) } -> Mark.Replacement

values.name: block
values.comment:
A named block.

    Mark.block "MyBlock"
        Html.text
        Mark.string

Will parse the following and render it using `Html.text`

    |> MyBlock
        Here is an unformatted string!

**Note** block names should be capitalized. In the future this may be enforced.
values.type: String.String -> (child -> result) -> Mark.Block child -> Mark.Block result

values.name: bool
values.comment:
Capture either `True` or `False`.

`elm-markup` doesn't infer truthiness in other values, so it needs to be exactly `True` or `False`.

values.name: commonReplacements
values.comment:
This is a set of common character replacements with some typographical niceties.

  - `...` is converted to the ellipses unicode character(`…`).
  - `"` Straight double quotes are [replaced with curly quotes](https://practicaltypography.com/straight-and-curly-quotes.html) (`“`, `”`)
  - `'` Single Quotes are replaced with apostrophes(`’`).
  - `--` is replaced with an [en-dash(`–`)](https://practicaltypography.com/hyphens-and-dashes.html).
  - `---` is replaced with an [em-dash(`—`)](https://practicaltypography.com/hyphens-and-dashes.html).
  - `<>` also known as "glue", will create a non-breaking space (` `). This is not for manually increasing space (sequential `<>` tokens will only render as one ` `), but to signify that the space between two words shouldn't break when wrapping. Like glueing two words together!

**Note** this is included by default in `Mark.text`

values.name: compile
values.type: Mark.Document meta data -> String.String -> Mark.Outcome (List.List Mark.Error.Error) (Mark.Partial ( meta, List.List data )) ( meta, List.List data )

values.name: document
values.comment:
Create a markup `Document`. You're first goal is to describe a document in terms of the blocks you're expecting. Here's an overly simple document that captures one block, a Title, and wraps it in some `Html` document : Mark.Document (Html msg) document = Mark.document (\title -> Html.main [] [ title ]) (Mark.block "Title" (Html.h1 []) Mark.string ) will parse the following markup: ```markup |> Title Here is my title! ``` and ultimately render it as ```html

Here is my title!

```

values.name: documentWith
values.comment:
Capture some metadata at the start of your document, followed by the body.

    import Mark

    Mark.documentWith
        { id = \metadata -> metadata.id
        , metadata =
            Mark.record
                (\id author publishedAt ->
                    { author = author
                    , publishedAt = publishedAt
                    }
                )
                |> Mark.field "id" Mark.string
                |> Mark.field "author" Mark.string
                |> Mark.field "publishedAt" Mark.string
        , blocks =
            [

            ]
        }

**Note** - You can also specify an `id`, which is a document identifier and is included in `Mark.Edit.Id`. This is really only necessary if you're building an editor that can edit multiple documents at once.

Otherwise, feel free to simple put `id = \_ -> "doc"`
values.type: { id : metadata -> String.String, metadata : Mark.Record metadata, blocks : List.List (Mark.Block block) } -> Mark.Document metadata block

values.name: field
values.type: String.String -> Mark.Block value -> Mark.Record (value -> result) -> Mark.Record result

values.name: idToString
values.comment:
It may be necessary to convert an `Id` to a `String` and back in order attach it as an `Html.Attributes.id` and read it back.

See the editor example for more details.

**Note** be aware that the actual string format of an `Id` is an implementation detail and may change even on patch releases of a library.

values.name: lookup
values.comment: Look up a specific block in your document by id.
values.type: Mark.Edit.Id -> Mark.Document meta block -> Mark.Parsed -> Mark.Outcome (List.List Mark.Error.Error) (Mark.Partial block) block

values.name: manyOf
values.comment: Many blocks that are all at the same indentation level.

values.name: map
values.comment: Change the result of a block by applying a function to it.

values.name: metadata
values.type: Mark.Document metadata document -> String.String -> Result.Result Mark.Error.Error metadata

values.name: onError
values.comment:
Parsing any given `Block` can fail.

However sometimes we don't want the _whole document_ to be unable to render just because there was a small error somewhere.

So, we need some way to say "Hey, if you run into an issue, here's a placeholder value to use."

And that's what `Mark.onError` does.

    Mark.int
        |> Mark.onError 5

This means if we fail to parse an integer (let's say we added a decimal), that this block would still be renderable with a default value of `5`.

**Note** If there _is_ an error that is fixed using `onError`, we'll get a [`Partial`](#Partial) when we render the document. This will let us see the _full rendered document_, but also see the _error_ that actually occurred.

values.name: parse
values.type: Mark.Document metadata data -> String.String -> Mark.Outcome (List.List Mark.Error.Error) (Mark.Partial Mark.Parsed) Mark.Parsed

values.name: record
values.comment:
Parse a record with any number of fields.

    Mark.record "Image"
        (\src description ->
            Html.img
                [ Html.Attributes.src src
                , Html.Attributes.alt description
                ]
                []
        )
        |> Mark.field "src" Mark.string
        |> Mark.field "description" Mark.string
        |> Mark.toBlock

would parse the following markup:

```markup
|> Image
    src = http://placekitten/200/500
    description = What a cutie.
```

Fields can be in any order in the markup. Also, by convention field names should be `camelCase`. This might be enforced in the future.

values.name: render
values.type: Mark.Document meta data -> Mark.Parsed -> Mark.Outcome (List.List Mark.Error.Error) (Mark.Partial ( meta, List.List data )) ( meta, List.List data )

values.name: replacement
values.comment:
Replace a string with another string. This can be useful to have shortcuts to unicode characters.

For example, we could use this to replace `...` with the unicode ellipses character: `…`.

values.name: string
values.comment:
This will capture a multiline string.

For example:

    Mark.block "Poem"
        (\str -> str)
        Mark.string

will capture

```markup
|> Poem
    Whose woods these are I think I know.
    His house is in the village though;
    He will not see me stopping here
    To watch his woods fill up with snow.
```

Where `str` in the above function will be

    """Whose woods these are I think I know.
    His house is in the village though;
    He will not see me stopping here
    To watch his woods fill up with snow."""

**Note** If you're looking for styled text, you probably want [`Mark.text`](#text) or [`Mark.textWith`](#textWith).

values.name: text
values.comment:
One of the first things that's interesting about a markup language is how to handle _styled text_.

In `elm-markup` there are only a limited number of special characters for formatting text.

  - `/italic/` results in _italics_
  - `*bold*` results in **bold**
  - and `~strike~` results in ~~strike~~

Here's an example of how to convert markup text into `Html` using `Mark.text`:

    Mark.text
        (\styles string ->
            Html.span
                [ Html.Attributes.classList
                    [ ( "bold", styles.bold )
                    , ( "italic", styles.italic )
                    , ( "strike", styles.strike )
                    ]
                ]
                [ Html.text string ]
        )

Though you might be thinking that `bold`, `italic`, and `strike` are not nearly enough!

And you're right, this is just to get you started. Your next stop is [`Mark.textWith`](#textWith), which is more involved to use but can represent everything you're used to having in a markup language.

**Note:** Text blocks stop when two consecutive newline characters are encountered.

values.name: textWith
values.comment:
Handling formatted text is a little more involved than may be initially apparent, but have no fear!

`textWith` is where a lot of things come together. Let's check out what these fields actually mean.

  - `view` is the function to render an individual fragment of text.
      - This is mostly what [`Mark.text`](#text) does, so it should seem familiar.
  - `replacements` will replace characters before rendering.
      - For example, we can replace `...` with the real ellipses unicode character, `…`.
  - `inlines` are custom inline blocks. You can use these to render things like links or emojis :D.
values.type: { view : Mark.Styles -> String.String -> rendered, replacements : List.List Mark.Replacement, inlines : List.List (Mark.Record rendered) } -> Mark.Block (List.List rendered)

values.name: toBlock
values.comment: Convert a `Record` to a `Block`.

values.name: tree
values.comment:
Would you believe that a markdown list is actually a tree?

Here's an example of a nested list in `elm-markup`:

```markup
|> List
    1.  This is definitely the first thing.

        With some additional content.

    --- Another thing.

        And some more content

        1.  A sublist

            With it's content

            And some other content

        --- Second item
```

**Note** As before, the indentation is always a multiple of 4.

In `elm-markup` you can make a nested section either `Bulleted` or `Numbered` by having the first element of the section start with `-` or `1.`.

The rest of the icons at that level are ignored. So this:

    |> List
        1. First
        -- Second
        -- Third

Is a numbered list. And this:

    |> List
        -- First
            1. sublist one
            -- sublist two
            -- sublist three
        -- Second
        -- Third

is a bulleted list with a numbered list inside of it.

**Note** You can use as many dashes(`-`) as you want to start an item. This can be useful to make the indentation match up. Similarly, you can also use spaces after the dash or number.

Here's how to render the above list:

    import Mark

    myTree =
        Mark.tree "List" renderList text

    -- Note: we have to define this as a separate function because
    -- `Items` and `Node` are a pair of mutually recursive data structures.
    -- It's easiest to render them using two separate functions:
    -- renderList and renderItem
    renderList (Mark.Enumerated list) =
        let
            group =
                case list.icon of
                    Mark.Bullet ->
                        Html.ul

                    Mark.Number ->
                        Html.ol
        in
        group []
            (List.map renderItem list.items)

    renderItem (Mark.Item item) =
        Html.li []
            [ Html.div [] item.content
            , renderList item.children
            ]
values.type: (Mark.Enumerated item -> result) -> Mark.Block item -> Mark.Block result

values.name: verbatim
values.comment:
A `verbatim` annotation is denoted by backticks(\`) and allows you to capture a literal string.

Just like `annotation`, a `verbatim` can have a name and attributes attached to it.

Let's say we wanted to embed an inline piece of elm code. We could write

    inlineElm =
        Mark.verbatim "elm"
            (\id str ->
                Html.span
                    [ Html.Attributes.class "elm-code" ]
                    [ Html.text str ]
            )

Which would capture the following

    Here's an inline function: `\you -> Awesome`{elm}.

**Note** A verbatim can be written without a name or attributes and will capture the contents as a literal string, ignoring any special characters.

    Let's take a look at `http://elm-lang.com`.

values.name: verify
values.comment:
`Mark.verify` lets you put constraints on a block.

Let's say you don't just want a `Mark.string`, you actually want a date.

So, you install the [`ISO8601`](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/rtfeldman/elm-iso8601-date-strings/latest/) and you write something that looks like:

    import Iso8601
    import Mark
    import Mark.Error
    import Time

    date : Mark.Block Time.Posix
    date =
        Mark.verify
            (\str ->
                str
                    |> Iso8601.toTime
                    |> Result.mapError
                        (\_ -> illformatedDateMessage)
            )
            Mark.string

    illformatedDateMessage =
        Mark.Error.custom
            { title = "Bad Date"
            , message =
                [ "I was trying to parse a date, but this format looks off.\n\n"
                , "Dates should be in ISO 8601 format:\n\n"
                , "YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss.SSSZ"
                ]
            }

Now you can use `date` whever you actually want dates and the error message will be shown if something goes wrong.

More importantly, you now know if a document parses successfully, that all your dates are correctly formatted.

`Mark.verify` is a very nice way to extend your markup however you'd like.

You could use it to

  - add units to numbers
  - parse a custom format, like [Latex mathematical equations](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/LaTeX/Mathematics#Operators)
  - ensure that numbers are between a range or are always positive.

How exciting! Seriously, I think this is pretty cool.
values.type: (a -> Result.Result Mark.Error.Custom b) -> Mark.Block a -> Mark.Block b

values.name: withAttr
values.comment:
Get an `Id` associated with a `Block`, which can be used to make updates through `Mark.Edit`.

        Mark.string
            |> Mark.withAttr
                (\str -> ("link", str))
values.type: (a -> ( String.String, String.String )) -> Mark.Block a -> Mark.Block a

values.name: withId
values.comment:
Get an `Id` associated with a `Block`, which can be used to make updates through `Mark.Edit`.

    Mark.withId
        (\id str ->
            Html.span
                [ onClick (Mark.Edit.delete id) ]
                [ Html.text str ]
        )
        Mark.string
values.type: (Mark.Edit.Id -> a -> b) -> Mark.Block a -> Mark.Block b

name: Mark.Edit

aliases.name: Id
aliases.comment: Every block has an `Id`. You can retrieve the `Id` for a block using [`Mark.withId`](Mark#withId)

aliases.name: Offset
aliases.comment:
The index of the rendered `String`. Let's say you have this string in your markup source.

```markup
Here is *my string*.
```

When you're rendering this, it's broken into three segments.

  - `Here is`
  - `my string` (which is bold)
  - `.`

The `Offset` is character position where those three strings are considered as one big one.

Here are some lookups:

  - `0  -> H`
  - `8  -> m`
  - `17 -> .`

We're not counting control characters from our markup source.

aliases.name: Styles
aliases.type: { bold : Basics.Bool, italic : Basics.Bool, strike : Basics.Bool }

values.name: addStyles
values.comment:
Add the given styles if they are not present in the text selection.

**Note:** Other styling is unaffected.

values.name: annotate
values.comment:
Put the current text selection within an `annotation` with some attributes.

Here's an example that would turn the selection into a `link`.

    Mark.Edit.annotate id
        start
        end
        "link"
        [ ( "url", Mark.New.string "https://guide.elm-lang.org/" ) ]

**Note** existing annotations within the selection are removed.
values.type: Mark.Edit.Id -> Mark.Edit.Offset -> Mark.Edit.Offset -> String.String -> List.List ( String.String, Mark.New.Block ) -> Mark.Edit.Edit

values.name: copyText
values.type: Mark.Edit.Id -> Mark.Edit.Offset -> Mark.Edit.Offset -> Mark.Internal.Description.Parsed -> List.List Mark.New.Text

values.name: delete
values.comment:
Delete blocks.

This may fail if the block cannot be deleted(such as a field in a record).

values.name: insertAfter
values.comment: Insert a block at an index within a `Mark.manyOf`.

values.name: insertFirst
values.comment:
Insert a block at the first position.

The id needed here is of the _parent_.

values.name: insertString
values.comment: This will work on both styled text and string datatypes.

values.name: removeStyles
values.comment:
Remove the given styles if they are present in the text selection.

**Note:** Other styling is unaffected.

values.name: replace
values.comment: The `Block` mentioned here is actually a `Mark.New.Block`. Use [`Mark.New`](/Mark-New) to create the new block you'd like.

values.name: restyle
values.comment: Remove all styling on the text selection and replace with the given styling.

values.name: update
values.type: Mark.Internal.Description.Document meta data -> Mark.Edit.Edit -> Mark.Internal.Description.Parsed -> Result.Result (List.List Mark.Error.Error) Mark.Internal.Description.Parsed

values.name: verbatim
values.comment:
Same as `annotate`, but creates a `verbatim` instead.

**Note** existing annotations within the selection are removed.
values.type: Mark.Edit.Id -> Mark.Edit.Offset -> Mark.Edit.Offset -> String.String -> List.List ( String.String, Mark.New.Block ) -> Mark.Edit.Edit

name: Mark.Error

aliases.name: Custom
aliases.comment: Create a custom error message to use with [`Mark.verify`](Mark#verify)

aliases.name: Details
aliases.type: { title : String.String, message : List.List Mark.Error.Text, region : Maybe.Maybe Mark.Error.Range }

aliases.name: Position
aliases.type: { offset : Basics.Int, line : Basics.Int, column : Basics.Int }

aliases.name: Text
aliases.type: { text : String.String, bold : Basics.Bool, underline : Basics.Bool, color : Maybe.Maybe String.String }

name: Mark.Internal.Description

unions.name: Block
unions.comment:
A `Block data` is just a parser that results in `data`.

You'll be building up your `Document` in terms of the `Blocks`.

A block starts with `|` and has a name(already built into the parser)

A value is just a raw parser.

--- Error Propagation

    Failure
        -> The document has had a mismatch
        -> This can only be recovered by `oneOf` or `manyOf`
    Almost
        -> An `Error` has been "raised".  It's propogated until it's resolved by `onError`
        ->

    Success
        -> Well, this is the easy one.

    Ids are only available on the element they apply to.

    Recovery is done at the level above the errored block.

    So, if there is a record with a string that is constrained

    If the constraint fails, the record can say "Here's the reset"
cases: { kind : Mark.Internal.Description.BlockKind, converter : Mark.Internal.Description.Description -> Mark.Internal.Description.BlockOutcome data, expect : Mark.Internal.Description.Expectation, parser : Mark.Internal.Description.ParseContext -> Mark.Internal.Id.Seed -> ( Mark.Internal.Id.Seed, Parser.Advanced.Parser Mark.Internal.Error.Context Mark.Internal.Error.Problem Mark.Internal.Description.Description ) }

unions.name: Description
cases: { id : Mark.Internal.Id.Id, name : String.String, found : Mark.Internal.Description.Description }
{ id : Mark.Internal.Id.Id, name : String.String, found : List.List ( String.String, Mark.Internal.Description.Description ) }
{ id : Mark.Internal.Id.Id, first : Mark.Internal.Description.Description, second : Mark.Internal.Description.Description }
{ id : Mark.Internal.Id.Id, icon : Mark.Internal.Description.Icon, content : List.List Mark.Internal.Description.Description, children : List.List Mark.Internal.Description.Description }

unions.name: Document
cases: { expect : Mark.Internal.Description.Expectation, metadata : Parser.Advanced.Parser Mark.Internal.Error.Context Mark.Internal.Error.Problem (Result.Result Mark.Internal.Error.UnexpectedDetails meta), parser : Parser.Advanced.Parser Mark.Internal.Error.Context Mark.Internal.Error.Problem Mark.Internal.Description.Parsed, converter : Mark.Internal.Description.Parsed -> Mark.Internal.Description.BlockOutcome ( meta, List.List data ) }

unions.name: InlineExpectation
cases: { name : String.String, kind : Mark.Internal.Description.InlineSelection, fields : List.List ( String.String, Mark.Internal.Description.Expectation ) }

unions.name: NewInline
cases: { name : String.String, kind : Mark.Internal.Description.InlineSelection, fields : List.List ( String.String, Mark.Internal.Description.New ) }

unions.name: Record
cases: { name : String.String, blockKind : Mark.Internal.Description.BlockKind, expectations : List.List ( String.String, Mark.Internal.Description.Expectation ), fieldConverter : Mark.Internal.Description.Description -> Mark.Internal.Description.InlineSelection -> Mark.Internal.Description.BlockOutcome (Mark.Internal.Description.FieldConverter data), fields : List.List Mark.Internal.Description.FieldParser }

unions.name: Text
unions.comment: A text fragment with some styling.

unions.name: Uncertain
unions.comment:
With this type, we're not quite sure if we're going to be able to render or not.

Scenarios:

  - A field value of a record has multiple errors with it.
  - It's caught and the error statue is rendered immediately.
  - The document can be rendered, but we still have multiple errors to keep track of.

aliases.name: ParsedDetails
aliases.type: { errors : List.List Mark.Internal.Error.Rendered, found : Mark.Internal.Description.Description, expected : Mark.Internal.Description.Expectation, initialSeed : Mark.Internal.Id.Seed, currentSeed : Mark.Internal.Id.Seed, attributes : List.List Mark.Internal.Description.Attribute }

aliases.name: Styling
aliases.type: { bold : Basics.Bool, italic : Basics.Bool, strike : Basics.Bool }

values.name: compile
values.type: Mark.Internal.Description.Document meta data -> String.String -> Result.Result (Mark.Internal.Outcome.Outcome (List.List Mark.Internal.Error.Rendered) { errors : List.List Mark.Internal.Error.Rendered, result : ( meta, List.List data ) } ( meta, List.List data )) ( Mark.Internal.Description.Parsed, Mark.Internal.Outcome.Outcome (List.List Mark.Internal.Error.Rendered) { errors : List.List Mark.Internal.Error.Rendered, result : ( meta, List.List data ) } ( meta, List.List data ) )

values.name: create
values.comment:
This function does no validation, it only creates a new Description.

    `
values.type: Mark.Internal.Id.Seed -> Mark.Internal.Description.New -> { desc : Mark.Internal.Description.Description, seed : Mark.Internal.Id.Seed }

values.name: createMany
values.comment: NOTE, this returns items in reversed order!
values.type: Mark.Internal.Id.Seed -> List.List Mark.Internal.Description.New -> { new : List.List Mark.Internal.Description.Description, seed : Mark.Internal.Id.Seed }

values.name: emptyRange
values.type: { start : { offset : number, line : number, column : number }, end : { offset : number, line : number, column : number } }

values.name: findMany
values.comment:
Subtle destinction between this and `find`.

This will NOT reconstruct the full hierarchy, but return the exact blocks asked for.

This also won't verify that all ids in the document were found, it'll just return the stuff it finds.

values.name: findMatch
values.type: Mark.Internal.Description.Description -> List.List (Mark.Internal.Description.Block a) -> Mark.Internal.Outcome.Outcome Mark.Internal.Error.AstError (Mark.Internal.Description.Uncertain (Mark.Internal.Description.WithAttr a)) (Mark.Internal.Description.WithAttr a)

values.type: Mark.Internal.Description.ParseContext -> Mark.Internal.Id.Seed -> Mark.Internal.Description.Block data -> ( Mark.Internal.Id.Seed, Parser.Advanced.Parser Mark.Internal.Error.Context Mark.Internal.Error.Problem Mark.Internal.Description.Description )

values.type: Mark.Internal.Description.ParseContext -> Mark.Internal.Id.Seed -> Mark.Internal.Description.Block data -> ( Mark.Internal.Id.Seed, Parser.Advanced.Parser Mark.Internal.Error.Context Mark.Internal.Error.Problem Mark.Internal.Description.Description )

values.name: lookup
values.comment:
In order for block lookups to work and for the block to be renderable, we need to retrieve a block, but keep the general structure.

So, if we have `ManyOf [ One,Two ]`, and our ID matches `Two`, we need to return `ManyOf [Two]`, not just `Two`.

Then our rendering function will still work.
values.type: Mark.Internal.Id.Id -> Mark.Internal.Description.Document meta block -> Mark.Internal.Description.Parsed -> Mark.Internal.Outcome.Outcome (List.List Mark.Internal.Error.Rendered) (Mark.Internal.Description.Partial block) block

values.name: matchExpected
values.comment: Is the first expectation a subset of the second?

values.name: mergeListWithAttrs
values.type: (List.List one -> List.List two -> final) -> Mark.Internal.Description.ListOutcome one -> Mark.Internal.Description.ListOutcome two -> Mark.Internal.Description.BlockOutcome final

values.name: mergeWith
values.type: (success -> a -> b) -> Mark.Internal.Outcome.Outcome failure (Mark.Internal.Description.Uncertain success) success -> Mark.Internal.Outcome.Outcome c (Mark.Internal.Description.Uncertain a) a -> Mark.Internal.Outcome.Outcome Mark.Internal.Error.AstError (Mark.Internal.Description.Uncertain b) b

values.name: mergeWithAttrs
values.comment: mergeWithAttrs
values.type: (one -> two -> final) -> Mark.Internal.Description.BlockOutcome one -> Mark.Internal.Description.BlockOutcome two -> Mark.Internal.Description.BlockOutcome final

values.name: render
values.comment:
Render is a little odd.

We should always expect `Parsed` to either already have our error messages, or to succeed.

Render can't add additional errors.
values.type: Mark.Internal.Description.Document meta data -> Mark.Internal.Description.Parsed -> Mark.Internal.Outcome.Outcome (List.List Mark.Internal.Error.Rendered) { errors : List.List Mark.Internal.Error.Rendered, result : ( meta, List.List data ) } ( meta, List.List data )

{ found : String.String, options : List.List String.String, recordName : String.String }
{ bold : Basics.Bool, italic : Basics.Bool, strike : Basics.Bool }

name: Mark.Internal.Id
comment: @docs Id, Seed, dedent, fromString, indent, initialSeed, step, thread, threadThrough, toString

unions.name: Seed
unions.comment:
We might want to move to a list based ID.

So, when we `indent`, we're actually adding a level.

    Seed [0]

    Seed [1] --indent -> Seed [1, 0]
                            |
                            v
                         Seed [1, 1]


    Seed [2]

Is there a performance issue with allocating a bunch of lists intead of base Ints?

A very common pattern is to

    1. step a seed forward and pass that seed for the next block
    2. indent the same original seed, and pass that to the children

So

    [0]
        -> stepped [1]  (goes to next element by handing the seed back in the parser)
        -> indented [0,0] (handed to child parser to go nuts with)

values.comment: Almost like `|=`, except

values.name: oneOf
values.comment:
A tolerant `oneOf` means it has to succeed.

So, you provide an error to return if everything else fails.
values.type: problem -> List.List (Mark.Internal.TolerantParser.Parser context problem data) -> Mark.Internal.TolerantParser.Parser context problem data

values.name: try
values.comment:
This might be a little odd, but it essentially means the parser its attached to should run normally, meaning it's ok if it fails.

This is generally useful with a `oneOf`, to ensure that an option can fail in the normal way and skip to try the next option.
values.type: Parser.Advanced.Parser c p d -> Mark.Internal.TolerantParser.Parser c p d

name: Mark.New
comment:
Create a new `Mark.Block` that can be added to your document using `Mark.Edit.replace` or `Mark.Edit.insertAt`.

Let's say we are capturing a basic diagram in our document and we want to dynamically insert a circle.

We can make a new circle by writing the following function

    import Mark.New as New

    type alias Circle =
        { x : Int
        , y : Int
        , label : String
        }

    circle : Circle -> Mark.New.Block
    circle details =
        New.record "Circle"
            [ ( "label", New.string details.label )
            , ( "x", New.int details.x )
            , ( "y", New.int details.y )
            ]

And then insert our newly made circle using `Mark.Edit.insertAt`.

**Note:** The document will only accept edits which are valid.

@docs Block


# Primitives

@docs string, int, float, bool


# Blocks

@docs block, record, many

@docs bullet, numbered


# Text

Here's an example of creating some text.

    newText =
        New.text
            [ Mark.unstyled "Look at my "
            , Mark.bold "cool"
            , Mark.unstyled " new text!"
            ]

@docs Text, text, unstyled, bold, italics, strike, Styles, styled


# Text Annotation

@docs annotation, verbatim

aliases.name: Styles

values.name: annotation
values.comment:
New.annotation
        { name = "link"
        , text =
            [ New.unstyled "my link to the elm website!" ]
        , fields =
            [ ( "url", Mark.string "https://elm-lang.com" ) ]
        }
values.type: { name : String.String, text : List.List ( Mark.New.Styles, String.String ), fields : List.List ( String.String, Mark.New.Block ) } -> Mark.New.Text

values.name: verbatim
values.type: { name : String.String, text : String.String, fields : List.List ( String.String, Mark.New.Block ) } -> Mark.New.Text
